title: Epic: multi-client — daemon, PWA, voice, VR
body:
Goal: multiple front-ends — a PWA on the phone, a desktop web view,
eventually a VR client — read and write the same project state.
End state: the user can open a project on the laptop, see the same
bugs from their phone, voice-input a new bug, and have it land on disk.

# Approach

Two big pieces, separable:

1. **The daemon.** `jjf serve` exposes the storage layer over an
   HTTP/WebSocket API. Reads are HTTP GETs; mutations are POSTs that
   round-trip to the same `Jjf-Op:` trailer flow the CLI uses.
   Streaming: live updates to bugs (a new comment, a status change)
   push to subscribers via SSE or WS so multiple clients stay in sync.
2. **The PWA.** A small JS/TS app that hits the daemon's API.
   Phone-first: large tap targets, dark mode, offline-capable.
   The voice-input piece (see `voice` below) lands here.

What we know we want, but haven't designed:

- **Voice input.** The iOS app's *useful* property is on-device
  speech-to-text better than Apple's built-in dictation. We don't
  want to ship a native iOS app; the PWA equivalent is a Whisper-class
  wasm model running in the browser. Whether that's `whisper.wasm`,
  `whisper-rs` compiled to wasm, or something else is open.
- **Server-side vs client-side transcription.** If on-device wasm is
  too slow or too large, fall back to sending audio to the daemon
  (which has more compute) and getting text back. The daemon-side
  path is also viable for a desktop client that lacks a microphone.
- **VR.** Far-future. The same API the PWA hits should be usable from
  a VR client (Unity, Bevy, whatever). The story is "point at an
  issue, see what the agent is doing, talk to it." Streaming is what
  makes this land.
- **Auth.** The daemon is local-only initially (laptop). When exposed
  to the LAN or beyond, needs a secret-key auth model similar to
  what goose's Lapstone does. Out of scope for v0.

Open architectural questions:

- HTTP vs HTTP+WS. The streaming case wants WS; the simple read/write
  case wants HTTP. Probably both.
- Daemon as a separate `jjforge-server` binary, or `jjf serve` in the
  same binary? Probably the latter for v1.
- Schema for the live-update stream. Probably JSON events shaped like
  the `Jjf-Op:` trailers; consumers reapply them locally.

# Child tickets we'll file

- `mc-daemon-skeleton` — `jjf serve`, HTTP routes for the core read verbs.
- `mc-daemon-mutations` — write verbs, transactional behavior.
- `mc-daemon-streaming` — WS subscription for live updates.
- `mc-pwa-skeleton` — vite + react app, manifest.json, add-to-homescreen story.
- `mc-pwa-bug-list` — render `jjf ls`.
- `mc-pwa-bug-detail` — render `jjf show`, comment threads.
- `mc-pwa-mutations` — create / comment / close from the PWA.
- `mc-voice-wasm-spike` — research spike: get Whisper-class transcription
  running in the browser. Decide on-device vs server-side after the spike.
- `mc-auth-secret-key` — when the daemon goes off-localhost.

# Depends on

- `mvp-cli` (the daemon shares the storage layer).
- `agent-ergonomics` (the live-update stream is most valuable for showing
  "what is the agent doing right now").

# Blocks

- The "ecosystem view" pitch from the project's vision: a website that's
  a view into many active agent sessions.

# Status

Sketched (2026-06-21). The MVP daemon is the next concrete work after
the merge driver lands; the PWA and voice are later. VR is sketched
only to make sure the API can support it later. Re-filed in jjforge
2026-06-22 during the git-bug cutover; the original ticket (`820998d`
in archived `refs/bugs/*`) had no status comments.
